perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information” shall mean any information belonging to a party or a related company (as hereinafter defined) which is not generally available to or used by others, or the utility or value of which is not generally known or recognized as a standard practice and may include without limitation any and all financial information; any and all employment information; any and all technical and non-technical information, including patent, copyright, trade secret and similar proprietary information; any information related to current, future and proposed business information, plans, activities, products and services, computer software, and other technology, including without limitation, forecasts, market research, development, design details, specifications, financial information, procurement requirements, purchasing, manufacturing, contractor and subcontractor lists, and sales and merchandising plans (including such information of each and any affiliate, subsidiary, or the like) in any medium whatsoever, whether oral, written, machine readable data, through facsimile, electronic mail, postal service or otherwise, provided by or disclosed either directly or indirectly by the Disclosing Party to the Receiving Party whether such information is designated as confidential at the time of delivery or not.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information” shall mean any information belonging to a party or a related company (as hereinafter defined) which is not generally available to or used by others, or the utility or value of which is not generally known or recognized as a standard practice and may include without limitation any and all financial information; any and all employment information; any and all technical and non-technical information, including patent, copyright, trade secret and similar proprietary information; any information related to current, future and proposed business information, plans, activities, products and services, computer software, and other technology.  Unless explicitly marked confidential, information disclosed will be treated as non-proprietary.
perturbation.explanation: The original text defines 'Confidential Information' broadly, regardless of whether it is designated as confidential at the time of delivery. The modified text adds a contradictory statement that information will be treated as non-proprietary unless explicitly marked confidential. This contradiction creates uncertainty about what constitutes 'Confidential Information' under the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 1.01 Definitions. In this Agreement, unless something in the subject matter or context is inconsistent therewith: “Agreement” means this Agreement and all amendments made hereto by written agreement between LEE&PAK and E-SMART. “Business Day” means any day except Saturday, Sunday and statutory holidays observed in the Country of Korea. “Disclosing Party” is the party who is disclosing Confidential Information to the other party. “Confidential Information” shall mean any information belonging to a party or a related company (as hereinafter defined) which is not generally available to or used by others, or the utility or value of which is not generally known or recognized as a standard practice and may include without limitation any and all financial information; any and all employment information; any and all technical and non-technical information, including patent, copyright, trade secret and similar proprietary information; any information related to current, future and proposed business information, plans, activities, products and services, computer software, and other technology. Unless explicitly marked confidential, information disclosed will be treated as non-proprietary. The term “Confidential Information” as used herein shall not include information: (I) which was generally available to or used by others, or the utility or value of such information is already generally known or recognized as a standard practice at the time it was communicated to the Receiving Party or subsequently becomes generally available or recognized as a standard practice through no fault or breach on the part of the Receiving Party; (ii) which the Receiving Party can demonstrate by a written or electronic document to have had rightfully in its possession free from any obligation of confidence at the time of disclosure; (iii) which the Receiving Party can demonstrate that it rightfully obtained free of any obligation of confidence subsequent to the time it was disclosed to the Receiving Party by the Disclosing Party; (iv) which the Receiving Party rightfully obtained from a third party who has the-right to transfer or disclose it ; (v) which the Receiving Party to whom it is disclosed hereunder can demonstrate was independently developed by such party or agents of that party without any use of the Confidential Information. “Effective Date” shall mean the 30 th day of May, 2008. “Receiving Party” is the party who is the recipient of the other party’s Confidential Information. “Related Company” shall mean any corporation, company, or other entity which at the time of disclosure of Confidential Information: a) is controlled by a party hereto; b) Controls a party hereto; c) or is under common Control with a party hereto. For this purpose, “Control ” means that more than fifty percent (50%) of the controlled entity’s shares or ownership interest representing the right to make decisions for such entity that are owned or controlled, directly or indirectly, by the controlling entity.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Confidential Information' as any information belonging to a party, regardless of whether it is designated as confidential. The changed text adds 'Unless explicitly marked confidential, information disclosed will be treated as non-proprietary.' This directly contradicts the original definition, creating an in-text contradiction as described in the Perturbation Explanation and Misaligned Terminology definition. The contradicted text is directly impacted by this change as it relies on the original definition of 'Confidential Information'.

perturbation.original_text: Each party shall only have the right to disclose the Confidential Information to its employees, agents and consultants on a “need to know” basis; provided, however, that disclosure in any event shall only be made to such persons who have agreed in writing to protect the confidentiality of the Disclosing Party’s information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party shall only have the right to disclose the Confidential Information to its employees and selected consultants on a “need to know” basis; provided, however, that disclosure in any event shall only be made to such persons who have agreed orally to protect the confidentiality of the Disclosing Party’s information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires a written agreement for confidentiality from anyone receiving Confidential Information. The change allows for oral agreements, contradicting the stricter requirement and weakening protection. This is contradictory because written agreements provide a stronger legal basis for enforcement than oral ones.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.04 Non-Disclosure. The Receiving Party agrees to use reasonable care, but in no event less than ordinary care, to prevent the unauthorized use, disclosure, publication or dissemination of the Confidential Information. Each party shall only have the right to disclose the Confidential Information to its employees and selected consultants on a “need to know” basis; provided, however, that disclosure in any event shall only be made to such persons who have agreed orally to protect the confidentiality of the Disclosing Party’s information. Each party shall, prior to disclosing any Confidential Information to any such person, issue appropriate instructions to them and obtain all necessary undertakings to ensure that such Persons comply with the confidentiality and use obligations and restrictions contained In this Agreement with respect to the Confidential Information. These obligations shall survive termination or expiry of this Agreement. The Receiving Party may Disclose Confidential Information if required by law, provided that the Receiving Party will take reasonable steps to give the Disclosing Party sufficient prior written notice of the requirement and provide reasonable assistance to enable the Disclosing Party to seek protection of its Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires written agreements for confidentiality, but the changed text allows for oral agreements. The contradicted text includes the changed text, which states that disclosure can be made to persons who have agreed orally to protect confidentiality. This directly contradicts the initial requirement of written agreements, creating a strong in-text contradiction by weakening protection where stricter terms were previously mandated. This aligns with the definition of 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 3.02 Termination. Either party may terminate this Agreement without cause, by giving the other party five (30) Business Days advance written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.02 Termination. Either party may terminate this Agreement without cause, by giving the other party five (10) Business Days advance written notice.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires 30 business days' notice for termination. The modification reduces it to 10. This directly contradicts the previous statement and creates ambiguity about the actual required notice period for termination.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.02 Termination. Either party may terminate this Agreement without cause, by giving the other party five (10) Business Days advance written notice.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated a 30-day notice period for termination, which was modified to a 10-day notice period. The contradicted text also states the termination notice is 10 business days. The perturbation explanation details how this change directly contradicts the original 30-day requirement, creating ambiguity and a clear conflict within the document regarding the required notice period, aligning with the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' definition.
